preFix.title: Polishing and protecting smooth leather shoes
preFix.subtitle: Clean, nourish, and polish leather shoes to a lasting finish
preFix.excerpt: How to polish and protect smooth leather shoes: removing surface dirt, conditioning the leather, applying wax polish in layers, bringing up a shine, and finishing with a weather-resistant top coat.
preFix.sourceNotes: Sweeney Todd, Bespoke: The Craft of the English Bootmaker.

content.text: Regular
wax polish

maintains the leather surface, seals out water, and slows the scuffing and cracking that ends the life of an otherwise sound shoe. The process runs: clean, condition, polish, buff, protect. Skipping the conditioner stage and going straight to wax starves the leather and causes premature cracking.
When to use it
Regular maintenance of smooth leather dress shoes, brogues, and boots.
Restoring colour and surface to scuffed or dull leather.
Preparing newly-purchased shoes before first wear.
Step-by-step
Step 1, clean the surface.

Remove the laces. Brush off loose dirt with a dry horsehair brush. Wipe the leather all over with a damp cloth to remove surface salt and grit. Do not soak the leather; a cloth wrung out thoroughly is damp enough.
Step 2, apply conditioner.

Work a small amount of leather conditioner (or a neutral cream polish) into the leather with a cloth, covering the whole surface including the welt seam and the back heel. Leave for five minutes to absorb. The conditioner replaces oils lost during wear and drying; leather that cracks is leather that was not conditioned.
Step 3, apply wax polish.

Take a small amount of wax polish on a soft cloth (a piece of old cotton is better than the foam dauber supplied in the tin) and work it into the leather in small circular strokes, building up a thin, even coat. Let the solvent dry for two minutes (the surface goes from shiny to dull) before applying the second coat. Two to three coats is sufficient for a standard service polish.
Step 4, buff to a shine.

Buff the dried wax briskly with a clean horsehair brush or a soft cloth. The friction melts the wax surface slightly, filling the grain and building the shine. Continue until the surface reaches the desired level. For a standard everyday shine this takes 30 to 60 seconds; for a
military shine
on the toe cap it takes considerably longer.
Common mistakes
Applying too much wax in one coat.
Thick coats dry to a dull, crumbly surface that will not buff. A thin coat applied and buffed gives more shine than a thick coat.
Using the wrong polish colour.

A neutral polish over time fades colour in dark leather; match the polish to the shoe colour, or use a transparent neutral for light tans. Black polish on a brown shoe is permanent and cannot be undone.
